summary: I was born in Thailand, specifically in the Umpiem Refugee Camps. My family fled to Thailand in 1999 after the Democratic Karen Buddhist Army burned down their village in Burma. Living in Thailand was difficult, especially because I only got to attend first grade before my mom decided we should move to the United States. Thailand was both a place of refuge and transition for me and my family.

My parents, sister, and grandparents lived in the Umpiem Refugee Camps in Thailand after fleeing Burma in 1999. I was actually born there while my mother was escaping from the violence caused by the Democratic Karen Buddhist Army. The camp was where my family stayed before we eventually moved to the United States. It was a place of safety but also hardship, especially with limited educational opportunities for me.

My parents, sister, and grandparents were originally from Burma. In 1999, the Democratic Karen Buddhist Army burned down their village there, forcing them to flee to Thailand. Burma is where my family’s roots are, but because of the conflict, we had to leave and seek safety in refugee camps. It was a painful part of our history that shaped our journey.

The Democratic Karen Buddhist Army, or DKBA, was responsible for burning down my family’s village in Burma in 1999. Because of their actions, my parents, sister, and grandparents had to escape and move to the Umpiem Refugee Camps in Thailand. Their violence changed the course of my family’s life and led to our long journey toward safety and eventually resettlement in the United States.

On April 29, 2008, my family and I traveled from Thailand to Mae Sot by taxi, which was my first time riding in one and made me feel dizzy. We stayed in Mae Sot for about five hours, where I remember seeing people selling food on the street and my mom buying meatballs for me and my sister. Mae Sot was an important stop on our journey from the refugee camp to the United States.

After Mae Sot, we traveled by double-decker bus to Bangkok, where we waited for about an hour. While waiting, I saw many refugee families also waiting for taxis. The hotel staff in Bangkok kindly gave us rice soup, which tasted delicious to me. Bangkok was another key point in our long trip before flying to the United States.

My mom decided to move our family to the United States for a better life and education. We left Thailand on April 29, 2008, and after stops in Mae Sot and Bangkok, we flew to New York City. The United States represented hope and new opportunities for me and my family after years of hardship in refugee camps.

When we arrived in New York City, we had to catch another plane to Georgia. I remember being very tired on the plane to New York and falling asleep, waking up to food I didn’t like. New York was a brief but important stop on our way to starting a new life in the United States.

After arriving in New York, we flew to Georgia and landed at Hartsfield–Jackson Atlanta International Airport. Our caseworker met us there and took us to a big apartment made of strong brick, which was a big change from the bamboo and leaves we were used to. I lived in Georgia for three years and was happy with the better living conditions, but my mom later decided to move us to Minnesota for better medical care.

We landed at Hartsfield–Jackson Atlanta International Airport when we arrived in Georgia after our flight from New York. It was the first place in Georgia where we saw our caseworker, who helped us get to our new apartment. That airport marked the beginning of our life in Georgia and the United States.

After living in Georgia for three years, my mom decided to move our family to Minnesota because the medical care in Georgia was not good. Now in Minnesota, I have better opportunities for education and a better life overall. Moving to Minnesota has been an important step in improving our health and future prospects.
